messages.content:
A scout kept a log while exploring an underground burrow network of
chambers joined by corridors. Corridors run both ways: the return
direction is always the exact reverse (north-south, east-west, up-down).

Exploration log:
1. From Stonelap we went east into Cinderpit.
2. From Cinderpit we went south into Quartzrun.
3. From Quartzrun we went east into Duskhollow.
4. From Duskhollow we went north into Grubline.
5. From Grubline we went down into Tallowmere.
6. From Tallowmere we went up into Grubline.
7. From Grubline we went west into Dripvault.
8. From Dripvault we went north into Gnarlden.
9. From Gnarlden we went west into Mirebend.
10. From Mirebend we went east into Gnarlden.
11. From Gnarlden we went south into Dripvault.
12. From Dripvault we went east into Grubline.

From Grubline, which single direction leads directly to Duskhollow?

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
The question asks which direction goes from Grubline straight into Duskhollow. Let me reconstruct the burrow map from the log first; every corridor runs both ways, with the return being the exact reverse.
Entry 1 maps Stonelap east to Cinderpit (and Cinderpit west back to Stonelap).
Entry 2: Cinderpit leads south to Quartzrun, so from Quartzrun the way back is north.
Entry 3: from Quartzrun, going east, we reach Duskhollow; the return corridor from Duskhollow runs west.
Entry 4 maps Duskhollow north to Grubline (and Grubline south back to Duskhollow). That's the very pair the question asks about.
Entry 5: Grubline leads down to Tallowmere, so from Tallowmere the way back is up.
Entry 6 walks the Tallowmere-Grubline corridor again; nothing new to map.
Entry 7 maps Grubline west to Dripvault (and Dripvault east back to Grubline).
Entry 8: Dripvault leads north to Gnarlden, so from Gnarlden the way back is south.
Entry 9: from Gnarlden, going west, we reach Mirebend; the return corridor from Mirebend runs east.
Entry 10 re-covers Mirebend to Gnarlden, already on my map.
Entry 11 retraces the corridor between Gnarlden and Dripvault that I already have.
Entry 12 walks the Dripvault-Grubline corridor again; nothing new to map.
Now I read my map at Grubline. Its known exits: south to Duskhollow, west to Dripvault, down to Tallowmere.
Of those, west and down lead elsewhere; the exit that reaches Duskhollow is south.
Note that entry 4 recorded this corridor from Duskhollow's side, going north. The reverse pairs are north-south, east-west, up-down, so from Grubline it must run south. That checks out.
So from Grubline, the single direction that leads directly to Duskhollow is south.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

2. drop before yeg

accepted too.)

SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Below is the BUGGY copy of a loom-house routine (exactly one line differs from the correct copy). Trace it exactly as printed.

1: ZERO B
2: SUB A 9
3: COPY A C
4: SUB D 8
5: ADD C 1
6: ZERO B
7: IFZ C
8: SUB A 9

Start registers: A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
After the routine finishes, what value does register C hold?

I begin with A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
The label says this is the buggy copy, but the question asks what this printed routine leaves in C, so I trace it exactly as shown.

Scanning first: only line 5 touches C, but an IFZ can change which lines actually run, so I trace everything in order.

Line 1, ZERO B — B is already 0 and stays 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Line 2, SUB A 9 — 0 minus 9 would go negative, so A floors at 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Line 3, COPY A C — A takes C's value, so A=0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Line 4, SUB D 8 — 7 minus 8 would go negative, so D floors at 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 5, ADD C 1 — C goes from 0 to 1. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.
Line 6, ZERO B — B is already 0 and stays 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.
Line 7, IFZ C — C is 1, not zero, so nothing is skipped. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.
Line 8, SUB A 9 — 0 minus 9 would go negative, so A floors at 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.
That was the last line, so the routine ends with A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.

Let me re-verify how C moved.
C begins at 0, then line 5 (ADD C 1) makes it 1, and it never changes again. That matches my trace.
So at the end of the routine, C holds 1.

messages.content:
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

This SPOOL routine was correct until exactly one line was changed
(the planted bug):

1: COPY C A
2: IFZ C
3: ZERO D
4: ADD C 1
5: ZERO C

Each test starts the registers as shown; 'correct' is what the
original routine yields, 'buggy' is what the routine above yields.

T1: start A=0 B=0 C=7 D=7 | correct A=7 B=0 C=0 D=7 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7 -> FAIL
T2: start A=6 B=8 C=0 D=2 | correct A=0 B=8 C=0 D=0 | buggy A=6 B=8 C=0 D=0 -> FAIL

Which line number holds the planted bug? (If replacing some other
single line would also make every test pass, that line number is
accepted too.)

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need to find the changed line. Plan: read the test diffs, trace a failing test to see what the routine really does, then try single-line replacements until every test passes.

T1 fails: A ends 0 but should be 7.
T2 fails: A ends 6 but should be 0.
So every failure lands on register A, though the miss differs between tests.

First I trace T1 through the shown routine.
Line 1 is COPY C A: C takes A's value, so C=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Line 2 is IFZ C: C is 0, so line 3 (ZERO D) is skipped. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Line 4 is ADD C 1: C goes from 0 to 1. Now A=0 B=0 C=1 D=7.
Line 5 is ZERO C: C clears from 1 to 0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7.
Final state A=0 B=0 C=0 D=7 — exactly the buggy finals listed for T1, so I am reading the routine correctly.

Now the substitution test: change one line in my head, rerun every test, and see whether all the finals match the correct column.

Now line 1 (COPY C A). Suppose it read SWAP A C instead.
T1 reruns to A=7 B=0 C=0 D=7 — the correct finals exactly.
T2 reruns to A=0 B=8 C=0 D=0, also correct.
One replacement at line 1 fixes every test at once.

Everything points to line 1 — that is where the bug sits.

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

E2: fole zim nack fole dree => zim zim nack nack nack | shown wheel gives zim zim hib hib hib

rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

ANSWER: <value>

messages.content:
You are repairing a routine for the loom-house's pattern engine.
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Exactly two lines of the routine were changed from the correct version; repair BOTH. Each test starts the registers as shown and lists the finals the CORRECT routine yields. Repair the routine so every test passes, then RUN to confirm.
Actions (one per turn):
PATCH <line#> <instruction> — replace that line (e.g. PATCH 3 ADD B 2)
RUN — rerun all tests and report pass/fail
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.

Buggy routine:

2: IFZ B
3: SUB D 5
4: SUB D 9
5: SUB C 6
6: SUB C 6
7: SUB A 2
8: COPY B C
9: SUB C 8
10: IFZ A
11: SWAP C D
12: ZERO D

T4: start A=0 B=1 C=0 D=0 | correct A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0 -> pass
PATCH 7 SUB A 7

(Use RUN to test.)

A scout kept a log while exploring an underground burrow network of
chambers joined by corridors. Corridors run both ways: the return
direction is always the exact reverse (north-south, east-west, up-down).

Exploration log:
1. From Mosshollow we went east into Wormcourt.
2. From Wormcourt we went up into Fernwell.
3. From Fernwell we went east into Cinderpit.
4. From Cinderpit we went east into Duskhollow.
5. From Duskhollow we went east into Palegrotto.
6. From Palegrotto we went down into Grubline.

Using only corridors named in the log, what is the shortest sequence of directions to go from Fernwell to Palegrotto?
Give the directions in order, separated by commas (for example: ANSWER: north, east).

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need the shortest sequence of directions from Fernwell to Palegrotto, using only corridors named in the log. First, the map: I'll take the log edge by edge, and since every corridor runs both ways, each entry also gives me the reverse direction back.
Entry 1: from Mosshollow, going east, we reach Wormcourt; return from Wormcourt runs west.
Entry 2 maps Wormcourt up to Fernwell (reverse: down).
Entry 3: Fernwell leads east to Cinderpit, so back from Cinderpit is west.
Entry 4: from Cinderpit, going east, we reach Duskhollow; return from Duskhollow runs west.
Entry 5 maps Duskhollow east to Palegrotto (reverse: west).
Entry 6: Palegrotto leads down to Grubline, so back from Grubline is up.
Map done. Now I search outward from Fernwell one move at a time — breadth-first — so the first time Palegrotto appears, that distance is the shortest possible.
One move out: from Fernwell I newly reach Cinderpit (east) and Wormcourt (down).
At 2 moves: from Cinderpit I newly reach Duskhollow (east); from Wormcourt I newly reach Mosshollow (west).
At 3 moves: from Duskhollow I newly reach Palegrotto (east).
Mosshollow only lead back to chambers I've already reached, so nothing new comes from there.
There it is: Palegrotto turns up at distance 3, entered from Duskhollow going east, and no earlier wave touched it, so 3 moves is the minimum.
Reading the route back off the search: from Fernwell go east to Cinderpit, then east to Duskhollow, then east to Palegrotto.
That's 3 moves, matching the distance.
So the shortest sequence of directions from Fernwell to Palegrotto is: east, east, east.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

Exactly ONE rule of this patchwheel was corrupted:

1. every zim becomes sabb
2. drop after dree
3. drop after zim
4. drop after tosk

Evidence (input => ORIGINAL wheel output):
E1: zim tosk sabb tosk zim => sabb tosk | shown wheel gives sabb tosk tosk
E2: fole sabb tosk dree zim => fole sabb tosk | shown wheel: match

Which rule number was corrupted? (If replacing a different single
rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

Rule 1 is every zim becomes sabb, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 2 is drop after dree, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 3 is drop after zim, giving sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 4 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk tosk.
That leaves sabb tosk tosk, but the original wheel gave sabb tosk.
My tape has 3 tokens; the original's has 2.
So the corruption changes how many tokens survive.

Since just one rule was tampered with, one well-chosen repair has to fix every example at once.
Rule 1 is a dead end: the tape reaching it in E1 is zim tosk sabb tosk zim, and no single replacement there makes all 2 examples match.
Rule 4 fails the same way.
Consider rule 3, currently 'drop after zim'. Test it as 'drop after tosk'.
Rules 1 and 2 are untouched, so the tape entering slot 3 is still sabb tosk sabb tosk sabb.
Rule 3 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk tosk.
Rule 4 is drop after tosk, giving sabb tosk.
Final: sabb tosk - exactly what the original wheel gave for E1.

Does that repair hold everywhere?
E2: fole sabb tosk dree zim comes out fole sabb tosk; the original gave fole sabb tosk - match.

With rule 3 restored, everything matches. The corrupted rule is number 3.

SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

This SPOOL routine was correct until exactly one line was changed
(the planted bug):

1: SUB B 3
2: ZERO A
3: IFZ B
4: SUB C 5
5: SUB D 3

Each test starts the registers as shown; 'correct' is what the
original routine yields, 'buggy' is what the routine above yields.

T1: start A=7 B=0 C=4 D=0 | correct A=0 B=0 C=4 D=3 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0 -> FAIL
T2: start A=7 B=0 C=2 D=1 | correct A=0 B=0 C=2 D=4 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=2 D=0 -> FAIL

Which line number holds the planted bug? (If replacing some other
single line would also make every test pass, that line number is
accepted too.)

ANSWER: <value>

T2 fails: D ends 0 but should be 4.
So every failure lands on register D, though the miss differs between tests.

First I trace T1 through the shown routine.
Line 1, SUB B 3 — 0 minus 3 would go negative, so B floors at 0. Registers: A=7 B=0 C=4 D=0.
Line 2, ZERO A — A clears from 7 to 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0.
Line 3, IFZ B — B is 0, so line 4 (SUB C 5) is skipped. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0.
Line 5, SUB D 3 — 0 minus 3 would go negative, so D floors at 0. Registers: A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0.
Final state A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0 — exactly the buggy finals listed for T1, so I am reading the routine correctly.

Time to try repairs: pick a suspect line, imagine a replacement there, and rerun all the tests mentally.

Now line 5 (SUB D 3). Suppose it read ADD D 3 instead.
T1 reruns to A=0 B=0 C=4 D=3 — the correct finals exactly.
T2 reruns to A=0 B=0 C=2 D=4, also correct.
One replacement at line 5 fixes every test at once.

So the planted bug is on line 5.

You stand before a sealed glyph gate. It transforms any string of exactly 5 glyphs by one fixed hidden rule. The glyph alphabet, in cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus (after lus the cycle wraps back to za). The hidden rule is ONE of: (a) the whole string is reversed; (b) the glyphs at two secret fixed positions trade places; (c) one secret fixed position is overwritten with one secret fixed glyph; (d) every glyph steps forward the same secret count along the alphabet cycle; (e) if the first glyph is in a secret pair of glyphs the whole string rotates one step left, otherwise one step right.
Goal: feed the gate an input that it transforms into the TARGET string.
Actions (one per turn):
  PROBE <glyph-string>  - spend one probe; the gate prints the transformed string
  OPEN <glyph-string>   - final commit; you succeed only if the gate's output equals the TARGET
Glyph strings are dash-joined, e.g. za-ke-ro. Probes are limited; OPEN ends the episode.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

Exactly ONE rule of this patchwheel was corrupted:

1. every yeg becomes varn
2. drop after varn
3. every ruv becomes varn
4. reverse all

Evidence (input => ORIGINAL wheel output):
E1: varn yeg yeg yeg plim => varn varn varn | shown wheel gives plim varn varn
E2: ulm varn plim plim ulm => ulm varn varn ulm | shown wheel gives ulm plim varn ulm

Which rule number was corrupted? (If replacing a different single
rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

Apply rule 1 (every yeg becomes varn): varn varn varn varn plim.
Apply rule 2 (drop after varn): varn varn plim.
Apply rule 3 (every ruv becomes varn): varn varn plim.
Apply rule 4 (reverse all): plim varn varn.
That leaves plim varn varn, but the original wheel gave varn varn varn.
The first difference is at slot 1: I have plim where it should be varn.
Same length, but the token identities are off.

Only one rule is corrupted, so I look for a slot where one replacement squares all the evidence.
Rule 1 is a dead end: the tape reaching it in E1 is varn yeg yeg yeg plim, and no single replacement there makes all 2 examples match.
Rule 2 fails the same way.
My suspicion is rule 3, shown as 'every ruv becomes varn'. What if the original was 'every plim becomes varn'?
Rules 1 and 2 are untouched, so the tape entering slot 3 is still varn varn plim.
Apply rule 3 (every plim becomes varn): varn varn varn.
Apply rule 4 (reverse all): varn varn varn.
Final: varn varn varn - exactly what the original wheel gave for E1.

Cross-check the rest with rule 3 repaired.
E2: ulm varn plim plim ulm comes out ulm varn varn ulm; the original gave ulm varn varn ulm - match.

That single repair squares all 2 examples. So the corrupted rule is rule 3.

messages.content:
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Below is the BUGGY copy of a loom-house routine (exactly one line differs from the correct copy). Trace it exactly as printed.

1: ADD B 2
2: ZERO B
3: SUB D 3
4: ZERO C
5: ZERO A
6: SWAP C B
7: COPY C A
8: SWAP D B

Start registers: A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
After the routine finishes, what value does register B hold?

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need to run this routine exactly as printed and report what register B holds when it finishes.
I begin with A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
The label says this is the buggy copy, but the question asks what this printed routine leaves in B, so I trace it exactly as shown.

Scanning first: lines 1, 2, 6, 8 touch B; I'll still trace the whole routine in order to keep every register straight.

Line 1 is ADD B 2: B goes from 0 to 2. Now A=4 B=2 C=5 D=0.
Line 2 is ZERO B: B clears from 2 to 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
Line 3 is SUB D 3: 0 minus 3 would go negative, so D floors at 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=5 D=0.
Line 4 is ZERO C: C clears from 5 to 0. Now A=4 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 5 is ZERO A: A clears from 4 to 0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 6 is SWAP C B: now C=0 and B=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 7 is COPY C A: C takes A's value, so C=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
Line 8 is SWAP D B: now D=0 and B=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.
That was the last line, so the routine ends with A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0.

Quick second pass on B to be safe.
B begins at 0, then line 1 (ADD B 2) makes it 2, then line 2 (ZERO B) makes it 0, and it never changes again. That matches my trace.
So at the end of the routine, B holds 0.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

4. drop after tosk

E3: quom ruv sabb quom hib hib quom => quom ruv hib quom hib hib quom | shown wheel: match

chambers joined by corridors. Corridors run both ways: the return

3. From Duskhollow we went south into Fernwell.
4. From Fernwell we went south into Quartzrun.
5. From Quartzrun we went south into Bramblehole.
6. From Bramblehole we went north into Quartzrun.
7. From Quartzrun we went south into Bramblehole.
8. From Bramblehole we went north into Quartzrun.
9. From Quartzrun we went north into Fernwell.

Entry 6 re-covers the corridor between Bramblehole and Quartzrun.

With the map rebuilt, I explore from Palegrotto in waves of one move each, so Bramblehole first shows up at its shortest distance.
One move out: from Palegrotto I newly reach Fernwell (west).
At 2 moves: from Fernwell I newly reach Duskhollow (north) and Quartzrun (south).
At 3 moves: from Quartzrun I newly reach Bramblehole (south).
Duskhollow only lead back to chambers I've already reached, so nothing new comes from there.
There it is: Bramblehole turns up at distance 3, entered from Quartzrun going south, and no earlier wave touched it, so 3 moves is the minimum.
Reading the route back off the search: from Palegrotto go west to Fernwell, then south to Quartzrun, then south to Bramblehole.
That's 3 moves, matching the distance.
So the shortest sequence of directions from Palegrotto to Bramblehole is: west, south, south.

ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

  ke-gor-gor-ro-fen-mu -> ke-gor-ti-ro-gor-mu
  mu-fen-ke-fen-ro-gor -> mu-fen-ti-fen-ke-gor

Try: first swap slots 3 and 5, then overwrite slot 3 with ti.
Probe 1: za-za-fen-ro-fen-mu -> za-za-fen-ro-fen-mu -> za-za-ti-ro-fen-mu -- matches the log.
Probe 2: ke-gor-gor-ro-fen-mu -> ke-gor-fen-ro-gor-mu -> ke-gor-ti-ro-gor-mu -- matches the log.
Probe 3: mu-fen-ke-fen-ro-gor -> mu-fen-ro-fen-ke-gor -> mu-fen-ti-fen-ke-gor -- matches the log.
Probe 4: za-za-mu-ro-fen-lus -> za-za-fen-ro-mu-lus -> za-za-ti-ro-mu-lus -- matches the log.
So the rule: first swap slots 3 and 5, then overwrite slot 3 with ti.
Now work backward from the target lus-mu-ti-fen-mu-za.
The overwrite forces slot 3 to ti, and lus-mu-ti-fen-mu-za indeed shows ti there. Before the overwrite that slot could have held anything; I take gor and keep the rest: lus-mu-gor-fen-mu-za.

So the answer is lus-mu-mu-fen-gor-za.

messages.content:
A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

Exactly ONE rule of this patchwheel was corrupted:

1. every quom becomes fole
2. rotate right
3. every zim becomes quom

Evidence (input => ORIGINAL wheel output):
E1: ulm ulm quom zim => quom ulm fole ulm | shown wheel gives quom ulm ulm fole
E2: ulm zim ulm ulm => ulm quom ulm ulm | shown wheel gives ulm ulm quom ulm

rule could also make every evidence tape match, that number is
accepted too.)

ANSWER: <value>

The first difference is at slot 3: I have ulm where it should be fole.
Same tokens, different order, so positions got scrambled.

Since just one rule was tampered with, one well-chosen repair has to fix every example at once.
Rule 1 is a dead end: the tape reaching it in E1 is ulm ulm quom zim, and no single replacement there makes all 2 examples match.
Rule 3 fails the same way.
Consider rule 2, currently 'rotate right'. Test it as 'swap first last'.
Rule 1 is untouched, so the tape entering slot 2 is still ulm ulm fole zim.
Rule 2 is swap first last, giving zim ulm fole ulm.
Rule 3 is every zim becomes quom, giving quom ulm fole ulm.
Final: quom ulm fole ulm - exactly what the original wheel gave for E1.

Does that repair hold everywhere?
